full_text:
*A Spatially Resolved Multi-Omics Atlas of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures
and Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes in Ovarian Cancer*

Authors: Zhihan Liang^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Hanna Elomaa^1^, Lina
Maltrovsky^1^, Joona Sarkkinen^2^, Iga Niemiec^1^, Aleksandra
Shabanova^1^, Anni Virtanen^1,4^, Matilda Salko^1^, Anniina
Färkkilä^1,3,5,6^

1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Helsinki, Finland

2 Translational Immunology Research Program, University of Helsinki and
Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

3 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology,
Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

5 iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland

6 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

**Introduction**

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes
(TILs) are key components of the tumor microenvironment, facilitating
local anti-tumor immune responses. TLSs presence has been linked to
improved outcomes in ovarian cancer, yet their spatial organization and
molecular characteristics remain underexplored. We constructed a

esponses. TLSs presence has been linked to
improved outcomes in ovarian cancer, yet their spatial organization and
molecular characteristics remain underexplored. We constructed a
spatially resolved multi-omics atlas by integrating histopathology,
transcriptomics, proteomics, and multiplexed imaging with genomic and
clinical data (homologous recombination status, PDS/IDS) to characterize
TLSs and TILs features, aiming to reveal their clinical associations and
underlying mechanisms in ovarian cancer.

**Material and method**

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) sections from over 200 Oncosys-Ova samples
were annotated to identify immune aggregates, immature TLSs, and mature
TLSs with germinal centers (GCs).

From these, 45 formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) high-grade serous
ovarian cancer (HGSC) omentum samples were selected for detailed spatial
single-cell, transcriptomic and proteomic profiling. All samples were
collected post-neoadjuvant chemotherapy during interval debulking
surgery, accompanied by clinical and WES + bulk RNA sequencing data.
t-CyCIF imaging was performed using a 30-marker immune antibody panel,
and adjacent sections underwent spatial transcriptomic profiling by
GeoMx, focusing on selected regions of interest (ROIs) within TLSs and
TILs. Proteomic profiling was conducted via proteo-picking, a novel

went spatial transcriptomic profiling by
GeoMx, focusing on selected regions of interest (ROIs) within TLSs and
TILs. Proteomic profiling was conducted via proteo-picking, a novel
microtissue extraction method, followed by mass spectrometry to identify
proteins across TLS compartments.

**Result and discussion**

Our analysis revealed heterogeneity in TLSs and TILs features across
ovarian cancer samples. Using an algorithm-assisted approach, H&E
annotation identified distinct immune aggregate patterns, with TLSs
maturity varying between patients. Notably, patients with poor prognosis
and homologous recombination proficiency lacked GCs, despite numerous
immune aggregates. Spatial metrics, including TLSs proximity to tumor,
were calculated to assess TLSs distribution and functionality across
molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.

Processed t-CycIF images were analyzed using SCIMAP and UTAG to uncover
functional immune composition within TLSs/TILs. Based on these results,
we selected 3 ROIs per TLSs on the adjacent sections: GCs, surrounding
T-B cells, and stromal areas, alongside another 5 ROIs per sample
representing immune aggregates.

In total, 268 ROIs were profiled by GeoMx to obtain spatial gene
expression data from TLSs/TILs compartments. These ROIs also guided
spatially matched proteomic analysis and achieved comprehensive
multi-omics data integration.

ain spatial gene
expression data from TLSs/TILs compartments. These ROIs also guided
spatially matched proteomic analysis and achieved comprehensive
multi-omics data integration.

**Conclusion**

Our spatially resolved multi-omics atlas reveals patient-specific
heterogeneity in TLS maturity, spatial organization, and immune
composition, uncovering mechanistic insights into immune activation
linked to clinical outcomes.
